sections.about: This metric measures spending on employee and worker wellbeing measures as a percentage of total revenue. It falls under BRSR Principle 3 — Employee Well-being, Essential Indicators. A higher value indicates better performance.
sections.performanceAndDrivers: In FY2022-23, SAIL led at 1.49%, followed by Jindal at 0.20% and Tata at 0.11%; JSW did not disclose FY2022-23 data. SAIL continues to dominate in FY2023-24 at 1.46% and FY2024-25 at 1.44%, far above Tata at 0.12% → 0.17%, JSW at 0.035% → 0.0253%, and Jindal at 0.03% → 0.05%. SAIL’s higher spend intensity likely reflects a large legacy workforce, township/medical ecosystem and public-sector welfare model, whereas private-sector peers may classify or centralise wellbeing costs differently.
sections.tataPositioning: In FY2024-25, Tata ranks 2nd at 0.17%, but remains well below SAIL’s 1.44%. Tata reported 0.11% in FY2022-23, 0.12% in FY2023-24 and 0.17% in FY2024-25, with its rank improving from 3rd in FY2022-23 to 2nd in FY2023-24 and FY2024-25. The improvement is positive, but the absolute gap to SAIL is large and may reflect both genuine welfare model differences and classification differences in what is counted as wellbeing spend.
sections.comparabilityNotes: FY2022-23 values for Tata (0.11%), SAIL (1.49%) and Jindal (0.2%) are prior-year comparatives reported in their respective FY2023-24 BRSRs; JSW did not disclose FY2022-23 data. Jindal’s FY2023-24 value is 0.03%, corrected from an originally disclosed 0.3% confirmed as a typographical error in Jindal’s FY2024-25 BRSR.
